profile_snapshot.user_summary: A research engineer in mechanistic interpretability whose core focus is sparse autoencoders (SAEs) as a practical tool for decomposing superposition in transformer activations. Their three seed papers — Towards Monosemanticity, Scaling Monosemanticity, and Cunningham et al. 2023 — place them squarely in the Anthropic-empirical tradition of dictionary learning for interpretability. They prioritize concrete, empirical results on real models: feature splitting behavior across SAE scales, alternative training objectives (Gated, TopK, JumpReLU, end-to-end KL+MSE) to replace L1, rigorous multi-metric evaluation of learned features (SAEBench-style), and downstream use of SAE features for circuit-level attribution. Their open question — feature transfer across model scales and fine-tunes — sits at a current empirical frontier addressed by only a handful of 2025–2026 papers. They explicitly don't want pure theory, RLHF methodology, capabilities-only work, or policy content.
profile_snapshot.current_question: How well do SAE features transfer across model scales and fine-tunes? Are there better SAE training objectives than L1?
interests: sparse autoencoder feature splitting at scale
SAE training objectives beyond L1 sparsity
gated TopK JumpReLU sparse autoencoder architectures
dictionary learning for language model interpretability
SAE evaluation benchmarks and proxy metrics
feature geometry in transformer residual stream
SAE feature transfer across model scales
SAE features for fine-tuned model analysis
sparse feature circuits for mechanistic explanation
scaling dictionary learning to frontier models

authors.name: Chris Olah
authors.why: Pioneer of transformer circuits and mechanistic interpretability; primary architect of the research tradition these seed papers belong to.

authors.name: Neel Nanda
authors.why: Leads DeepMind mech interp team; SAEBench co-author; most prolific empirical SAE researcher with broad output on features, circuits, and evaluation.

authors.name: Trenton Bricken
authors.why: Lead author on Towards Monosemanticity; foundational empirical SAE work showing dictionary learning recovers monosemantic features.

authors.name: Adly Templeton
authors.why: Lead author on Scaling Monosemanticity; key empirical work extracting millions of features from Claude 3 Sonnet at production scale.

authors.name: Adam Jermyn
authors.why: Core Anthropic circuits team; co-authored Scaling Monosemanticity and attention-layer mechanistic work.

authors.name: Hoagy Cunningham
authors.why: Lead author on the foundational SAE-interpretability paper (arXiv:2309.08600); early empirical proof that SAEs recover interpretable features.

authors.name: Lee Sharkey
authors.why: Co-authored foundational SAE paper; ongoing work on SAE geometry, canonical units critique, and training objectives at Goodfire.

authors.name: Joshua Batson
authors.why: Co-authored Towards Monosemanticity; interpretability team lead; featured in multiple transformer-circuits updates through 2025.

authors.name: Callum McDougall
authors.why: Co-authored the 2025 circuit tracing paper using attribution graphs; key figure bridging SAE features to circuit-level explanations.

authors.name: Adam Karvonen
authors.why: Lead author of SAEBench; most comprehensive empirical evaluation of SAE training objectives, architectures, and proxy metric reliability.

authors.name: Sam Marks
authors.why: SAEBench co-author; empirical work on SAE feature quality, evaluation methodology, and downstream task performance.

authors.name: Nora Belrose
authors.why: Key research on SAE feature inconsistency across training runs and open-source automated interpretability for SAE features.

authors.name: Dan Braun
authors.why: End-to-end SAE training and attribution-based parameter decomposition; directly relevant to L1 alternatives and functional feature identification.

authors.name: Stefan Heimersheim
authors.why: Empirical work on identifying functionally important SAE features using e2e training; alternative dictionary learning objectives.

authors.name: Tom Lieberum
authors.why: SAE research at scale on Gemma models; circuit tracing work from DeepMind mech interp team; relevant to feature transfer questions.

sources.name: Transformer Circuits Thread
sources.why: Anthropic's primary publication venue for mechanistic interpretability; verified active through May 2026 with circuit tracing, attention, and SAE-adjacent posts.

sources.name: Goodfire Research
sources.why: SAE-focused interpretability lab with papers published through May 2026 on feature geometry, manifold steering, and parameter-space interpretation.

sources.name: Apollo Research Publications
sources.why: Key source for end-to-end SAE training, functionally important feature identification, and mechanistic description length work; verified active March 2026.

sources.name: EleutherAI Blog
sources.why: Active through April 2026; posts on SAE feature consistency across training runs, automated interpretability, and alternative sparse coding architectures.

sources.name: Neuronpedia
sources.why: Open interpretability platform hosting 50M+ SAE latents, SAEBench comparisons, and SAE releases from Anthropic, DeepMind, and OpenAI — essential empirical workspace.

sources.name: DeepMind Safety Research Blog
sources.why: DeepMind mech interp team updates including SAE negative results and the pivot to pragmatic interpretability — important critical counterpoint on SAE limitations.

sources.name: MATS Research
sources.why: Outputs from MATS scholars; origin of SAEBench and several SAE evaluation papers from the independent mech interp community.

sources.name: Neel Nanda's Website
sources.why: Research index and explainers from the most prolific active SAE researcher; canonical reference and pointer to his team's ongoing work.

filter_outs: Pure theory papers with no empirical evaluation
capabilities work that doesn't bear on interpretation
profile_snapshot.notes: The three seed papers form a tight cluster — all empirical SAE-for-interpretability work in the Anthropic tradition, spanning a one-layer proof-of-concept (Cunningham et al.), the canonical large-scale SAE paper (Towards Monosemanticity), and the frontier scale-up to Claude 3 Sonnet (Scaling Monosemanticity) — confirming a dictionary-learning-first, empiricism-first style. The current question about feature transfer and L1 alternatives is well-supported by active 2025–2026 literature (SAEBench, JumpReLU, e2e SAE, MetaSAEs, the canonical-units critique), so the interests list was tuned to retrieve both the mainstream and the critical/evaluative work. Sources were chosen to cover four active poles of this community: Anthropic-empirical (transformer-circuits.pub), commercial interpretability labs (Goodfire), mechanistic-safety labs (Apollo), and open-source community (EleutherAI blog, Neuronpedia, MATS); all URLs were fetched and confirmed active in 2025–2026.

kept.title: Interpreting Language Model Parameters (adVersarial Parameter Decomposition / VPD)
kept.summary: Introduces VPD, which decomposes weight matrices into rank-one parameter subcomponents that are causally ablatable in isolation on a 67M-parameter transformer. The approach is activation-agnostic, claims to avoid SAE feature splitting by construction, and now handles attention layers.
kept.why_kept: Parameter-decomposition method that decomposes a 67M transformer into causally ablatable rank-one subcomponents and claims to avoid SAE feature splitting by construction; Goodfire/Apollo paper by followed authors Lee Sharkey and Dan Braun; on SAE training objectives beyond L1.

kept.title: A Mechanistic Investigation of Supervised Fine Tuning
kept.summary: Finds that SFT leaves residual-stream cosine similarity very high but reveals significant divergence when activations are projected through a base-model SAE, with sparse latents diverging substantially. Introduces a pipeline attributing which SAE features change most during SFT.
kept.discovered_via: openalex:dictionary learning for language model i
kept.why_kept: Uses base-model SAEs to show that SFT leaves residual-stream cosine similarity high while sparse latents diverge substantially, and attributes which features shift most; arXiv preprint directly on the user's current question of SAE features under fine-tuning.

kept.title: Qwen-Scope: Turning Sparse Features into Development Tools for Large Language Models
kept.summary: Releases Qwen-Scope, an open-source suite of 14 SAE groups across 7 Qwen model scales, with tools for feature visualization, steering, and cross-version comparison.
kept.discovered_via: openalex:SAE features for fine-tuned model analys
kept.why_kept: Open-source release of matched SAEs across 7 Qwen model scales with cross-version comparison tooling; arXiv preprint and community resource directly enabling SAE feature transfer studies across model scales.

kept.title: Natural Language Autoencoders Produce Unsupervised Explanations of LLM Activations
kept.summary: Trains two coupled Claude models — one to compress an activation into natural-language text, another to reconstruct the activation from the text — jointly optimized via RL. The resulting NLAs produce unsupervised, vocabulary-free explanations of activations and detected covert evaluation awareness in Claude Opus 4.6. Explicitly contrasted with SAEs as a fixed-dictionary alternative.
kept.why_kept: Anthropic paper training paired Claude models to verbalize and reconstruct activations via RL, replacing fixed sparse dictionaries with natural language and detecting safety-relevant states in Opus 4.6; on transformer-circuits.pub; on automated SAE feature interpretation.

kept.title: Causal Dimensionality of Transformer Representations: Measurement, Scaling, and Layer Structure
kept.summary: Introduces causal dimensionality κ(L,M,T) as the effective rank of the expected Jacobian outer product, estimated via SAE width sweeps with attribution patching. Across 7 SAE widths (16K–1M) on Gemma-2-2B, representational capacity grows 15.6× while causal capacity grows only 4×.
kept.why_kept: Measures effective causal rank across 7 SAE widths (16K–1M) on Gemma-2-2B and finds representational capacity grows 15.6× while causal capacity grows only 4×; arXiv preprint on SAE evaluation benchmarks and proxy metrics.

kept.title: Negation Neglect: When models fail to learn negations in training
kept.summary: Shows that fine-tuning on documents that flag a claim as false makes models more likely to believe the claim is true, a consistent phenomenon across negation, epistemic qualifiers, and misalignment warnings in all tested models.
kept.why_kept: Reports that fine-tuning on documents flagging a claim as false can systematically make the claim more believed across all tested models; LW post ▲96/27 comments co-authored by followed author Adam Karvonen; relevant to SAE features for fine-tuned model analysis.

kept.title: The Rate-Distortion-Polysemanticity Tradeoff in SAEs
kept.summary: Formalizes a three-way tension between reconstruction fidelity, rate, and monosemantic representations in SAEs; proves and empirically confirms in toy models that forcing monosemanticity incurs reconstruction cost.
kept.why_kept: Formalizes and empirically confirms a three-way tradeoff between reconstruction, rate, and monosemanticity in SAEs; arXiv preprint quantifying the limit any L1-style sparsity objective faces.

kept.title: Tree SAE: Learning Hierarchical Feature Structures in Sparse Autoencoders
kept.summary: Proposes Tree SAE, which learns parent–child feature hierarchies during training by combining an activation-coverage condition with a semantic consistency constraint, showing the coverage condition alone produces semantically unrelated false-positive parents.
kept.why_kept: Builds parent–child feature hierarchies into the SAE objective via a semantic consistency constraint added to activation coverage; arXiv preprint on feature splitting and SAE training objectives beyond L1.

kept.title: Bilinear autoencoders find interpretable manifolds
kept.summary: Replaces linear SAE latents with quadratic (bilinear) forms that decompose activations into low-rank quadratic components, compose linearly in weight space, and admit input-independent geometric analysis; finds qualitatively different concepts than linear SAEs.
kept.why_kept: Replaces linear SAE latents with bilinear quadratic forms that admit input-independent geometric analysis and detect qualitatively different concepts than linear SAEs; arXiv preprint on SAE training objectives beyond L1 and feature geometry.

kept.title: Descriptive Collision in Sparse Autoencoder Auto-Interpretability: When One Explanation Describes Many Features
kept.summary: Identifies 'descriptive collision' — many distinct SAE features receiving the same auto-generated explanation — as a systematic failure mode, structurally distinct from polysemanticity. Reanalyzes the largest public human-annotated SAE feature dataset to quantify prevalence.
kept.why_kept: Identifies and quantifies 'descriptive collision' (many distinct SAE features sharing one auto-generated explanation) on the largest public human-annotated SAE feature dataset; arXiv preprint on SAE evaluation benchmarks and automated feature interpretation.

kept.title: Activation Differences Reveal Backdoors: A Comparison of SAE Architectures
kept.summary: Compares Crosscoders and Differential SAEs (Diff-SAE) on their ability to isolate backdoor-related features in fine-tuned models, using a controlled SQL-injection backdoor as the testbed.
kept.discovered_via: openalex:dictionary learning for language model i
kept.why_kept: Head-to-head empirical comparison of Crosscoders and Differential SAEs on localizing features that change between base and fine-tuned models using a controlled SQL-injection backdoor; arXiv preprint on SAE features for fine-tuned model analysis.

kept.title: The World Inside Neural Networks: How neural geometry will unlock understanding and control of AI
kept.summary: Argues NNs develop internal geometric structures mirroring real-world relational structure (circular months, smooth spatial curves) and that SAE features 'shatter manifolds into many small and apparently-unrelated pieces.' Proposes manifold-based interpretability as a complement/alternative to feature-direction approaches. Companion repo 'sae-manifold' updated May 1 2026.
kept.why_kept: Goodfire programmatic essay arguing SAE features shatter the manifold structure NNs actually learn, proposing manifold-based interpretability instead; backed by a companion sae-manifold GitHub repo; on feature geometry in transformer residual stream.

kept.title: The Geometric Wall: Manifold Structure Predicts Layerwise Sparse Autoencoder Scaling Laws
kept.summary: Argues layer-to-layer variation in SAE reconstruction error is explained by geometric mismatch between the activation manifold's curvature/intrinsic dimensionality and the linear dictionary assumption; introduces a per-layer geometric diagnostic predicting where SAE scaling saturates.
kept.discovered_via: openalex:dictionary learning for language model i
kept.why_kept: Introduces a per-layer geometric diagnostic that predicts where SAE reconstruction error saturates, attributing scaling variation to activation-manifold curvature; arXiv preprint on SAE evaluation and feature geometry.

kept.title: An Introduction to Exemplar Partitioning for Mechanistic Interpretability
kept.summary: Argues SAEs bundle two distinct commitments — reconstruction fidelity and L1 sparsity over a fixed dictionary — that are appropriate for reconstructive decomposition but not for finding interpretable structure. Proposes exemplar partitioning, grouping activations by behavioral similarity instead.
kept.why_kept: Argues SAEs conflate reconstruction with L1 sparsity over a fixed dictionary and sketches exemplar partitioning by behavioral similarity as an alternative; LW post ▲63/6 comments on SAE training objectives beyond L1.

kept.title: fmxcoders: Factorized Masked Crosscoders for Cross-Layer Feature Discovery
kept.summary: Shows standard crosscoders largely fail to recover genuinely cross-layer features — despite evenly distributed decoder weight norms, each latent is effectively driven by only 1–2 layers — and proposes factorized masked crosscoders that enforce cross-layer coherence.
kept.discovered_via: openalex:dictionary learning for language model i
kept.why_kept: Shows standard crosscoder latents are effectively driven by 1–2 layers despite even decoder norms and proposes a factorized masked variant enforcing cross-layer coherence; arXiv preprint on dictionary learning for language model interpretability.
